planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade, setting 2–3 inches deep with growing tip up; white trillium is slow to establish but persists for decades once settled — never dig from the wild.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade, setting the rhizome 2–3 inches deep with the growing tip pointed upward; white trillium establishes slowly but once settled it requires essentially no care and will persist for decades. Trillium grandiflorum is the showiest and most beloved of the eastern North American trilliums, emerging in early spring with a whorl of three broad, dark-green leaves and a single, large, pure-white three-petaled flower up to 3 inches across that ages to pink as it matures; it is the provincial floral emblem of Ontario and one of the finest native wildflowers for shaded garden borders. Never dig plants from the wild — purchase nursery-propagated divisions only, as the plant takes 5–7 years to bloom from seed and wild populations are seriously threatened by overcollection and deer browsing. It naturalizes slowly under deciduous trees alongside ferns, wild ginger, and Virginia bluebells.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; seeds need cold winter stratification and germinate in early spring — sow fresh seed immediately after harvest as viability drops rapidly when dry.

directions: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; seeds need cold winter stratification and germinate in early spring — fresh seed sown immediately after harvest in late spring also works well as seeds lose viability quickly when dry. Bloodroot is a spring ephemeral native to eastern North American deciduous forests; it emerges very early in spring, often while snow still lingers, with a large, deeply lobed, blue-green leaf wrapped around a single bud that unfurls into a brilliant, pure-white flower with 8–12 petals and a golden center lasting only a day or two but spectacularly beautiful. The thick rhizome exudes bright red-orange latex sap when cut (giving rise to its common name) and the plant goes completely dormant by early summer; pair with hostas or ferns to fill the void. Plant has toxic sap; handle rhizomes with gloves.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall or early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; set rhizomes horizontally 2–3 inches deep and space generously as mayapple spreads vigorously by rhizomes to form large colonies.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall or early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; set rhizomes horizontally 2–3 inches deep and space generously as mayapple spreads vigorously by rhizomes to form large colonies. Podophyllum peltatum is a bold-textured native perennial of eastern North American forests; each plant sends up one or two large, deeply lobed, umbrella-like leaves up to 12 inches across, and the paired-leaf stems produce a single, nodding, white, waxy flower with 6–9 petals in April–May hidden beneath the leaves, followed in summer by a lemon-sized, yellowish, edible fruit (when fully ripe) with a sweet, tropical flavor that was used by Native Americans. All parts of the plant except the ripe fruit are toxic; wear gloves when handling rhizomes and do not allow children to eat unripe fruits. Mayapple naturalizes rapidly and makes an outstanding large-scale groundcover under deciduous trees.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in full to partial shade; blue cohosh is slow to establish but extremely long-lived once settled and is best left undisturbed after the first season.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in full to partial shade; blue cohosh is slow to establish but extremely long-lived once settled, and is best left undisturbed after the first season. Caulophyllum thalictroides is an elegant eastern North American native woodland perennial that emerges with a striking blue-green color in spring, bearing large, deeply compound leaves resembling meadow rue that give the plant a delicate, airy texture; small, inconspicuous yellowish-green to purplish flowers appear in early spring before the leaves fully expand, followed by ornamental clusters of deep blue, berry-like seeds (actually exposed ovules, not true fruits) in late summer and fall that persist well into winter. The plant has significant ethnobotanical history but all parts are toxic — do not ingest. Use it as an elegant foliage accent in shaded native plant gardens and woodland borders.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall immediately after harvest in moist, rich, neutral to slightly alkaline woodland or floodplain soil in partial to full shade; seeds require cold stratification over winter and germinate in early spring — do not allow seeds to dry out before sowing as viability drops rapidly.

directions: Direct sow fresh seed in fall immediately after harvest in moist, rich, neutral to slightly alkaline floodplain or woodland soil in partial to full shade; seeds require cold stratification over winter and germinate in early spring — do not allow seeds to dry out before sowing as viability drops rapidly. Virginia bluebells is the finest eastern North American spring ephemeral wildflower; it emerges with smooth, blue-green, glaucous foliage in early spring and produces arching clusters of trumpet-shaped flowers that open pink from the bud and mature to a luminous, sky-blue color in April and May, creating a breathtaking drift of color beneath flowering redbuds and dogwoods. The plant goes completely dormant by early summer, leaving no trace; mark its location and plant late-emerging hostas, ferns, or annuals to fill the gap. It naturalizes prolifically along stream banks and moist woodland floors and is a premier pollinator resource for native bumblebees.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall or early spring at the margins of ponds, streams, or rain gardens in consistently moist to saturated, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; marsh marigold thrives with its roots in shallow standing water up to 4–6 inches deep.

directions: Plant divisions in fall or early spring at the margins of ponds, streams, or rain gardens in consistently moist to saturated, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; marsh marigold thrives with its roots in shallow standing water and can be grown as a true aquatic marginal in up to 4–6 inches of water. Caltha palustris is one of the earliest and most cheerful native spring wildflowers of North American and European wetlands, producing clumps of large, glossy, kidney-shaped, deep-green leaves and brilliant, waxy, golden-yellow, buttercup-like flowers 1–2 inches across in April and May that are among the most vivid yellows in the early spring garden. The plant goes dormant in summer heat; it pairs beautifully with other wetland perennials including blue flag iris, cardinal flower, and lizard's tail for a season-long native water garden display. All parts are mildly toxic if eaten raw.
